patches.rationale: Fabricated citation: PNAS DOI returns 404. Real paper is in Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors.
Fabricated citation: Earth-Science Reviews paper not in Crossref. Real paper is Frontiers in Earth Science. Also fixes diacritical Pávón → Pavón.

patches.replace: the dome's primary geometric (firmament-height) function H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)
patches.rationale: H(r) is the firmament-HEIGHT function, not a geomagnetic field equation. Corrects mislabeling.

patches.find: a centre near 80°W drifting westward at 0.2–0.3°/yr
patches.replace: a centre near 65–75°W drifting westward at 0.2–0.3°/yr
patches.rationale: WMM/CHAOS-7 outputs place American-cell SAA centroid at 60–70°W, not 80°W. Updated to 65–75°W range.

patches.find: The 45°W threshold sits ~35° east of the current cell centre — the cell would have to reverse direction and travel 35° east to falsify it
patches.replace: The 45°W threshold sits ~20–30° east of the current cell centre — the cell would have to reverse direction and travel 20–30° east to falsify it
patches.rationale: Companion fix: with centre at 65–75°W, the gap to 45°W is 20–30°, not ~35°.

patches.find: Non-discriminating: Chapman 1933 + modern Sq models (Kim & Chang 2018) predict eclipse geomagnetic signals without dome geometry. Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation; its FSF formula uses the 2017 BOU observation as its own baseline — the absolute signal magnitude is observation-anchored, not dome-derived.
patches.replace: Non-discriminating: Chapman 1933 + modern Sq models (Kim & Chang 2018) predict eclipse geomagnetic signals without dome geometry. The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) and derives the station-to-station FSF = H(r)/r ratio from its own geometry (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999); what it does not derive is the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor, which is imported from the 2017 observation and reused as the 2026 baseline — so the predicted signal is observation-anchored, not dome-derived.
patches.rationale: EXP-228: WIN010-finding — concede equation and FSF derivation exist; keep critique on observation-anchored absolute magnitude.

patches.find: The dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations; it simply observes that the signal exists and claims it.
patches.replace: The dome has no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters; its published formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes the magnitude as an input from the 2017 observation it is trying to predict.
patches.rationale: EXP-228: WIN010-dvt-P1S4 — closes P1/P2 internal contradiction. P1 no longer claims dome has no equation.

patches.find: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 observations track FSF more tightly than they track eclipse shadow coverage (Besselian-elements geometry, the standard-model prediction). Kim & Chang's ensemble shows the Y-component and X/Z/F responses track shadow geometry at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, not disc-radius geometry. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if delta_Z tracks shadow coverage more tightly than FSF, the dome's scaling is post-hoc; if FSF beats coverage on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.replace: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), after factoring out the shadow-coverage term both models share — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. Both the dome formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and a standard-physics prediction ≈ baseline × coverage × f(mag_lat) include coverage as a factor, so coverage is not the discriminator — the real fingerprint is geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat). We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track magnetic-latitude scaling more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats mag-lat scaling on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.rationale: EXP-228: WIN010-dvt-P7 — reframes discriminator as FSF vs mag-lat scaling (not FSF vs coverage). Coverage is shared by both models and is not discriminating.

patches.find: Eclipse-induced ±10 nT magnetic variations are predicted by standard ionospheric Sq current physics (Chapman 1933). The dome provides no derived magnitude or mechanism
patches.replace: Eclipse-induced ±10 nT magnetic variations are predicted by standard ionospheric Sq current physics (Chapman 1933; Kim & Chang 2018). The dome derives the station-to-station FSF ratio from its own geometry but not the absolute magnitude, which is imported from the 2017 BOU observation and reused as the 2026 baseline input
patches.rationale: EXP-229: WIN025-finding — concede FSF ratio derivation consistent with WIN-010/WIN-068 cluster. Kim & Chang 2018 added for cluster consistency.

patches.find: Eclipse-induced geomagnetic variations of 5–30 nT have been predicted by standard ionospheric Sq current physics since Chapman (1933). The dome provides no independent derivation or mechanism — it relabels a 90-year-old standard prediction as ‘aetheric.’ The dome's refraction cascade further undermines the FSF derivation claim: if the dome's own n(r) medium makes the sun's physical position seasonally indeterminate, any FSF ratio anchored to dome-coordinate geometry inherits that indeterminacy (see Section 1.8). WIN-025 shares this mechanism with WIN-010 and WIN-068 — counting the same eclipse geomagnetic observation three times inflates the WIN count without adding independent evidence.
patches.replace: Eclipse-induced geomagnetic variations of 5–30 nT have been predicted by standard ionospheric Sq current physics since Chapman (1933) and quantitatively confirmed across the 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054). The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) and the FSF = H(r)/r station-to-station ratio is a genuine geometric derivation from dome parameters (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999, verified independently in the WIN-010/WIN-068 cluster) — we retract our earlier characterization of FSF as empirical. What the dome does not derive is the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor; that number is imported from the 2017 observation and reused as the 2026 baseline input. Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse) shows the disc-radius values FSF feeds on are themselves computed from observed Polaris elevation angles that travel through the same n(r) medium the dome concedes is 300–6,000× more refractive than Earth's atmosphere, so every dome-coordinate r in the FSF table inherits that contamination. WIN-025 shares this mechanism with WIN-010 and WIN-068 — counting the same eclipse geomagnetic observation three times inflates the WIN count without adding independent evidence.
patches.rationale: EXP-229: WIN025-dvt — updates vocabulary to cluster-consistent (Section 1.8 Coordinate Collapse, n(r) contamination framing, FSF geometric derivation concession).

patches.find: A = 62,376 nT and C = 64,852 nT closely match observed magnetic pole field strengths: ~54,000–56,000 nT at the North Magnetic Pole and ~55,000 nT at the geographic south pole per WMM2025.
patches.replace: A = 62,376 nT and C = 64,852 nT closely match observed pole-region field strengths: ~55,000 nT at the geographic north pole and ~55,000 nT at the geographic south pole per WMM2025 / IGRF-13. Both coefficients overshoot the geographic pole values by ~13–18% (A=62,376 is ~13% above 55,000; C=64,852 is ~18% above 55,000).
patches.rationale: EXP WIN-053 c9 H2: symmetrize comparators. Old text compared A to North Magnetic Pole (~54–56k) and C to geographic south pole (~55k), mixing pole types. Now both are compared to geographic poles (both ~55k per WMM2025).

patches.find: 6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than geodetic standards; AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology
patches.replace: 6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy; same-hemisphere routes (e.g. Australia to UK) also diverge at 6.2%, which V13 predicts should be its best case. AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology
patches.rationale: ISS-1434/ISS-1431: Replace misleading 10,000× figure with defensible framing and lead with same-hemisphere anomaly per curmudgeon c3 H5 recommendation.

patches.find: The 6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than standard geodetic accuracy — making this the dome's strongest evidence also its most damning comparison.
patches.replace: The 6.2% RMSE is roughly 6× worse than typical sub-meter GPS positioning on 4,000 km baselines, and orders of magnitude worse than millimeter-precision geodetic standards — making this the dome's strongest evidence also its most damning comparison.
patches.rationale: ISS-1431/ISS-1428: Replace misleading 10,000×–250,000× comparison with correct magnitude. 6.2% of 4,000 km = 248 km; sub-meter GPS is 1 m / 248 km ≈ 4 ppm; geodetic VLBI is mm-scale.

patches.find: Fail-clause concession that “signal explained by ionospheric Sq variation alone” would falsify the claim admits two natural readings, and the dome does not disambiguate between them.
patches.replace: Our codified Fail-clause — the phrase “signal explained by ionospheric Sq variation alone” that we appended to our Pass band — admits two natural readings, and the dome has not published any falsification criterion specific enough to disambiguate (PRED-095 as registered in data/predictions.json contains no Chapman-language Fail criterion at all, so any fair-audit Fail-clause inherits the ambiguity below).
patches.rationale: EXP-230: SEC8b.3-failclause-opening — owns the codification authorship. Old text attributed Fail-clause to dome; it is our codification.

patches.find: The dome does not publish which reading governs, and under either reading the Pass band fails to distinguish dome from globe — reading A surrenders discrimination to preserve consistency; reading B attempts discrimination by making Pass and Fail incompatible.
patches.replace: The dome has not committed to either disambiguation, and under either reading the Pass band fails to distinguish dome from globe — reading A surrenders discrimination to preserve consistency; reading B attempts discrimination by making Pass and Fail incompatible.
patches.rationale: EXP-230: SEC8b.3-failclause-closing — neutral phrasing; does not imply dome authored the codification.
